YAAKOV — CHAPTERS 26 THROUGH 35

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 26 Yaakov in Paddan-Aram — Leah and Rachel

1 And in the second year of this week Yaakov came to the land of the east, to Lavan the brother of Rivkah his mother, and he was with him, and he served him for Rachel his daughter one week of years.

2 And in the first year of the third week he said unto him: Give me my wife, for whom I have served you seven years.

3 And Lavan said unto Yaakov: I will give you your wife.

4 And Lavan made a feast, and took Leah his elder daughter and gave her to Yaakov as a wife, and gave her Zilpah his handmaid for a handmaid.

5 And Yaakov did not know, for he thought that she was Rachel, but it was morning, and behold it was Leah, and Yaakov was wroth with Lavan, and said to him: Why have you dealt crookedly with me?

6 Did I not serve you for Rachel and not for Leah? Why have you wronged me?

7 Take your daughter from me, and I will go — for you have done evil to me.

8 For Yaakov loved Rachel more than Leah — for Leah's eyes were weak, but her form was very handsome, but Rachel had beautiful eyes and a beautiful and very handsome form.

9 And Lavan said to Yaakov: It is not so done in our country, to give the younger before the elder.

10 And it is not right to do this, to give your younger daughter before your elder daughter — fulfil the week of this one, and I will give you that one also, that you may serve me another seven years, that you may pasture my sheep as before.

11 And Yaakov did so, and fulfilled the week of Leah, and Lavan gave him Rachel also to be his wife, and Bilhah his handmaid to be her handmaid.

12 And he served yet other seven years for Rachel.

13 And Rachel bore no children to Yaakov, and Leah bore Reuven to Yaakov in the first year of the third week, and Shim'on in the third year, and Levi in the sixth year, and Yehudah in the fourth week in the first year thereof.

14 And Bilhah the handmaid of Rachel bore Dan in the fifth year of the third week, and Naftali in the seventh year thereof.

15 And Zilpah the handmaid of Leah bore Gad in the first year of the fourth week, and Asher in the third year thereof.

16 And Yaakov was in the house of Lavan four weeks of years and one week and two years, and in the third year of the fifth week Yissakhar and Zevulun and Dinah were born.

17 And the River Nile flows from the mountains of Asshur and Kush, and the River Jordan flows into the sea of the Arabah.

18 And in the first year of the fifth week, Yosef was born.

19 And in the third year of the sixth week of this jubilee Rachel bore Binyamin.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserves portions of Chapter 26 in Hebrew. The precise jubilee-year dating of each son's birth is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 26 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 26:13-19 — the births of the twelve sons of Yaakov are placed within precise jubilee-year datings. Yovelim organizes the birth order of the twelve sons into the covenant calendar framework, making each birth a covenant event with a specific place in sacred time. The list of the twelve sons — Reuven, Shim'on, Levi, Yehudah, Dan, Naftali, Gad, Asher, Yissakhar, Zevulun, Yosef, and Binyamin — is the foundation of the twelve-tribe covenant structure that organizes all subsequent Yisra'ELi history.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 27 Yaakov's Return — The Meeting with Esav

1 And in the sixth year of this week Yaakov sent to his father-in-law Lavan that he should release him, and Lavan released him freely.

2 And he gathered all his belongings and possessions — both the cattle which were his, and the cattle which he had acquired in Paddan-Aram, and he went to his father Yitzhak, to the land of Kena'an.

3 And Lavan sent and took back his daughters and followed after Yaakov, and Yaakov did not know that Lavan had pursued after him.

4 And Lavan overtook Yaakov at the mountain Gal'ed.

5 And YHWH appeared to Lavan the Syrian in a dream at night, and said to him: Take heed that you speak not to Yaakov neither good nor evil.

6 And Lavan spake to Yaakov and said: What have you done? You have dealt deceitfully with me, and have driven away my daughters as captives taken with the sword.

7 Why did you flee secretly, and steal away from me, and did not tell me? I would have sent you away with joy and with songs, with timbrel and with harp.

8 And you did not even permit me to kiss my sons and my daughters — you have now done foolishly.

9 And now it is in my power to do you harm, but YHWH the Creator of your father said unto me last night: Take heed that you speak not to Yaakov either good or evil.

10 And now you have surely gone, because you so greatly longed after your father's house — but why have you stolen my deities?

11 And Yaakov answered and said to Lavan: Because I was afraid, for I said: Perhaps you would take your daughters from me by force.

12 With whomsoever you find your deities, he shall die — before our brethren point out what is yours with me and take it.

13 For Yaakov did not know that Rachel had stolen them.

14 And Lavan searched and found them not in Yaakov's tent and in Leah's tent and in the tent of the two handmaids, and he went into Rachel's tent and Rachel had taken the teraphim and put them in the camel's saddle, and sat upon them.

15 And Lavan searched all the tent and found them not, and she said to her father: Let not my lord be angry that I cannot rise up before you, for the way of women is upon me.

16 And he searched and found them not.

17 And Yaakov was wroth with Lavan, saying: What is my trespass? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued after me?

18 For twenty years have I been with you — your ewes and your she-goats have not cast their young, and the rams of your flocks have I not eaten.

19 That which was torn of beasts I brought not to you — I bore the loss of it. Of my hand did you require it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.

20 Thus I was — in the day the drought consumed me, and the frost by night, and my sleep departed from mine eyes.

21 These twenty years have I been in your house — I served you fourteen years for your two daughters, and six years for your cattle.

22 And they made a covenant — Yaakov and Lavan — and Lavan went back to his place and Yaakov went on his way.

23 And Yaakov sent messengers to his brother Esav, and they came to him, and Esav came to meet him with four hundred men.

24 And Yaakov was greatly afraid, and he divided the people that was with him, and the flocks, and the herds, and the camels, into two bands.

25 And Yaakov prayed to YHWH, saying: O Creator of my father Avraham and Creator of my father Yitzhak, YHWH who said unto me: Return to your country and to your kindred, and I will deal well with you.

26 I am not worthy of the least of all the mercies, and of all the truth, which you have shown unto your servant — for with my staff I passed over this Yarden, and now I am become two bands.

27 Deliver me, I pray, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esav — for I fear him, lest he will come and smite me, and the mother with the children.

28 And you said: I will surely do you good, and make your seed as the sand of the sea, which cannot be numbered for multitude.

29 And Yaakov lodged there that night, and he wrestled with an angel till the breaking of the day.

30 And he said unto the angel: Let me go, for the day breaketh.

31 And the angel said to him: What is your name? And he said Yaakov.

32 And the angel said: Your name shall be called no more Yaakov, but Yisra'EL, for you have striven with angels and with men, and have prevailed.

33 And Yaakov asked the angel, saying: Tell me, I pray, your name. And the angel said: Why is it that you ask after my name? And he blessed him there.

34 And Yaakov called the name of that place Peni'el, for he said: I have seen the face of YHWH and my life is preserved.

35 And the sun rose upon him as he passed over Penuel, and he was halting upon his thigh.

36 Therefore the children of Yisra'EL eat not of the sinew which is upon the hollow of the thigh, unto this day, because he touched the hollow of Yaakov's thigh in the sinew that shrank.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserves portions of Chapter 27 in Hebrew.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 27 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 27:32 — your name shall no more be called Yaakov, but Yisra'EL — for you have striven with angels and with men and have prevailed. The renaming of Yaakov to Yisra'EL is the covenant name-change that defines the entire people. Just as Avram became Avraham and Sarai became Sarah at the covenant of circumcision, Yaakov becomes Yisra'EL at the wrestling match with the angel. The covenant people take their name from this nocturnal struggle — Yisra'EL — he who strives with The Creator. The name carries the full weight of the covenant relationship: not passive submission but active engagement, wrestling, prevailing.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 28 The Rape of Dinah — Shim'on and Levi's Vengeance

1 And in the first year of the forty-fourth jubilee Yaakov went to Shalem, a city of Shekhem, which is in the land of Kena'an, in peace.

2 And Shekhem the son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the land, saw her, and he took her and lay with her and defiled her.

3 And she was a little girl, a child of twelve years.

4 And Hamor his father came to speak to Yaakov about Dinah, to take her to wife for his son Shekhem.

5 And Yaakov sent and called his sons from the field, and they came, his twelve sons, and he told them what Shekhem had done to Dinah their sister.

6 And the sons of Yaakov were very wroth because Shekhem had lain with Dinah their sister — for he had defiled her, and it was a great shame in Yisra'EL — and a thing not done in Yisra'EL, that any man should lie with his daughter.

7 And Shim'on and Levi answered in deceit and said to Hamor and his son Shekhem: We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to an uncircumcised man, for that were a reproach unto us.

8 Only on this condition will we agree to you: if you will be as we are, to circumcise every male among you, as we are circumcised.

9 Then will we give our daughters unto you, and we will take your daughters to us, and we will dwell with you and become one people.

10 But if you will not hearken unto us, to be circumcised, we will take our daughter, and we will depart.

11 And their words pleased Hamor and Shekhem, and they circumcised every male.

12 And it came to pass on the third day, when they were in pain, that two sons of Yaakov, Shim'on and Levi, took each man his sword and came upon the city boldly, and slew all the males.

13 And they slew Hamor and his son Shekhem, and took Dinah from the house of Shekhem, and they went out.

14 And the sons of Yaakov plundered the city of Shekhem, and took all the flocks and all the cattle and all that was in the city and in the field.

15 And all the women and children they took captive.

16 And Yaakov was very wroth at Shim'on and at Levi, and said unto them: Why have you done this? Behold you have made me to stink among all the inhabitants of the land — among the Kena'anites and the Perizzites.

17 And being few in number, they will assemble against me and slay me, and I shall be destroyed, I and my house.

18 And they said: Should he deal with our sister as with a harlot?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserves portions of Chapter 28 in Hebrew.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 28 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Dinah narrative in Yovelim expands the Bereshit 34 account with specific details — Dinah's age as twelve years, the characterization of the act as a great shame and a thing not done in Yisra'EL. Yovelim frames Shim'on and Levi's vengeance within the purity theology that runs throughout the book — the defilement of a covenant daughter by an uncircumcised Hivite is the precise violation of the endogamy principle that Rivkah commanded in Chapter 25. The violence of the response is presented not as disproportionate but as the covenant community's obligation to defend its daughters from this category of violation.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 29 Yaakov Goes to Bethel — The Death of Devorah

1 And Yaakov went up to Bethel in the third year of this week, on the new moon of the month.

2 And he built an altar at the place where he had slept, and he erected a pillar there, and he sent away the strange deities and the amulets and the rings and the idols which Rachel had stolen from Lavan her father, and he gave them to the sons of Yaakov, and they buried them under the oak which is near Bethel.

3 And in those days Rachel died, and she was buried in the way to Ephrath, that is Bethlehem.

4 But Yaakov built a pillar on the grave of Rachel, on the road above her grave.

5 And he journeyed from thence and came to the tower of Eder, and he dwelt there.

6 And Reuven went and lay with Bilhah, the concubine of his father, and Yaakov heard thereof.

7 And after this, Devorah the nurse of Rivkah died, and she was buried below Bethel in the valley under the oak of the valley.

8 And Yaakov called the name of that place the River of Devorah.

9 And YHWH appeared to Yaakov at the place where his wife Rachel died, in the land of Kena'an, in the third year of the first week of the forty-fifth jubilee.

10 And YHWH said unto him: Your name shall not be called Yaakov, but Yisra'EL shall they call your name.

11 And he said unto him: I am YHWH who created the heaven and the earth, and I will increase you and multiply you exceedingly — and kings shall come forth from you, and they shall judge everywhere where the foot of the sons of men has trodden.

12 And I will give to you and to your seed after you the land on which you are lying, and you shall take possession of it throughout all eternity.

13 And I finished speaking with him, and I went up from him, and Yaakov looked till I had gone up from him.

14 And he arose from the place where he had lain, and set up a pillar of stone in that place.

15 And he poured oil thereon, and he called the name of that place Bethel.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserves portions of Chapter 29 in Hebrew.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 29 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 29:2 — Yaakov sent away the strange deities and amulets and rings and idols which Rachel had stolen from Lavan, and buried them under the oak near Bethel. The radical purification of the covenant household at Bethel — burying all foreign religious objects under the oak — is the communal covenant renewal that follows the Shekhem episode. Bethel is the place where Yaakov first met YHWH in Chapter 25. The return to Bethel is a return to the covenant foundation, and it requires the burial of everything incompatible with exclusive YHWH worship. The idols Rachel stole from her father Lavan — the household deities of Paddan-Aram — have no place at the covenant altar.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 30 The Law Against Intermarriage — The Heavenly Tablets Record

1 And in the first year of the first week of the forty-fifth jubilee he went from thence and came to Hebron, and dwelt there.

2 And in the fourth year of the first week he went to the land of Shiloh, and dwelt there.

3 And Yaakov remembered the dream which he had dreamed at Bethel, and he arose and went to Bethel.

4 And YHWH appeared to him at night, and blessed him, and said to him: I am YHWH the Creator of Avraham your father and the Creator of Yitzhak — the land whereon you lie I will give to you and to your seed.

5 And I will multiply your seed as the sand of the sea, and your seed shall inherit the gates of those who hate them — and in you shall all families of the earth be blessed.

6 Therefore, I command and testify to you today that you should not give any of your daughters to their sons, and that you should not take any of their daughters for your sons, for it is contemptible before YHWH.

7 Because of their fornication and uncleanness and all their evil ways, Yisra'EL will be forsaken and become alien in the eyes of YHWH if they mingle with these peoples of the land.

8 For if any man in Yisra'EL wishes to give his daughter or his sister to any man who is of the seed of the nations, he shall surely die — they shall stone him, for he has wrought shame in Yisra'EL, and they shall burn the woman with fire.

9 Because she has dishonored the name of the house of her father, and she shall be rooted out of Yisra'EL.

10 Let there be no harlot nor any unclean woman in Yisra'EL, let Yisra'EL be holy unto YHWH, and let it not be defiled.

11 And let the judgment be executed upon the man who has defiled his daughter, as upon a fornicator — for he has caused a shame to be wrought in Yisra'EL.

12 And there is no forgiveness or pardon for this to atone for it forever — but he shall be purged who has dishonored his daughter.

13 And Yisra'EL will not be desolated or destroyed, for YHWH is in its midst, and his holy angels watching over it.

14 And to Shim'on and Levi was it given as an everlasting blessing — for they were zealous and they kept the commandments and the ordinances which YHWH commanded.

15 It is written and ordained on the heavenly tablets: Righteousness, blessing, and mercy and gladness — great joy is written down for all the seed of Yisra'EL who observe this forever.

16 And for all those who become impure and defile themselves, or who have united themselves with the daughters of the nations, there shall be no pardon or forgiveness.

17 For their defilement and their shame shall be blotted out from before YHWH and become a judgment upon them forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubf (4Q221) and 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserve portions of Chapter 30 in Hebrew. The intermarriage law and its heavenly tablet basis are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 30 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 30:6-8 — YHWH commands that no daughter of Yisra'EL shall be given to the sons of the nations, and no daughter of the nations shall be taken for the sons of Yisra'EL — and the man who violates this shall be stoned. The intermarriage law of Yovelim is the most stringent covenant-endogamy statement in the entire ancient Hebrew literature. It goes beyond Devarim 7's prohibition of specific Kena'anite marriages to a universal prohibition on all inter-national marriage within the covenant community. The Qumran community's strict halakhah on marriage reflects direct dependence on Yovelim's absolute standard.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 31 Yaakov Blesses Levi and Yehudah — The Priestly and Royal Lines

1 And he went up with Leah his wife to his father Yitzhak — Yitzhak his father was at the tower of Avraham, and Rivkah his mother was there also.

2 And Yitzhak and Rivkah rejoiced greatly because they saw Yaakov and all his sons coming, and they blessed him.

3 And Yaakov brought to Yitzhak everything that he had brought — cheese and butter and milk and fat and honey and dates and figs and pomegranates and all other fruits.

4 And he brought the tithes of everything to his father Yitzhak, and Yitzhak gave the tithe of everything to YHWH, and he blessed YHWH for sending Yaakov back in peace to see him twice before he died.

5 And Yitzhak took the tithe from Yaakov and presented it to YHWH and said: This is the tithe of my son's possessions — may YHWH grant you great peace and long life.

6 And Yitzhak called Levi his son and commanded him, saying: I command you, my son, to be zealous for YHWH and to walk in all his paths.

7 And command your children to keep the service of YHWH and to walk in his ways always, for YHWH has chosen you from all mankind to approach him, to serve in his sanctuary and to be like the angels of the presence, to be holy and to be pure.

8 And he blessed Levi and his sons, and said: May YHWH give to you and to your seed greatness and great glory, and cause you and your seed to draw near to him from all flesh to serve in his sanctuary as the angels of the presence and as the holy ones.

9 Even as they the seed of your sons shall be for glory and greatness and holiness — may he make them holy and may he be a father to them, and may they be his children.

10 And then Yitzhak called Yehudah, and said: May YHWH give you power and strength and be a blessing.

11 May YHWH give you majesty and glory, and rule over those who hate you — and may your sons be many in your days, and may you be as a root in the midst of the land.

12 And the nations shall fear you, and all the nations shall quake.

13 In you shall be the help of Yaakov, and in you shall be found the salvation of Yisra'EL.

14 When you sit on the throne of honor of your righteousness, there shall be great peace for all the seed of the beloved sons of The Creator.

15 Blessed be he that blesseth thee, and cursed be all they that hate and afflict you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubf (4Q221) preserves portions of Chapter 31 in Hebrew. The blessing of Levi and Yehudah by Yitzhak is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 31 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 31:7-8 — Yitzhak commands Levi to be zealous for YHWH and blesses his seed to serve in the sanctuary like the angels of the presence and the holy ones. The angelic comparison for the Levitical priesthood — already introduced in the circumcision chapter where the angels were created circumcised — is here made explicit in Yitzhak's blessing. The priests who serve in the sanctuary are functioning in the same capacity as the angels of the presence who serve before YHWH's throne. The human sanctuary is the earthly mirror of the heavenly throne room, and the Levitical priests are the earthly correspondents of the heavenly angels of the presence.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 31:14 — when you sit on the throne of honor of your righteousness, there shall be great peace for all the seed of the beloved sons. Yehudah's blessing in Yovelim establishes the royal-messianic trajectory for the tribe of Yehudah — the throne of righteousness, the greatness and glory of rule. Levi receives the priesthood and Yehudah receives the kingship. The two covenant offices of priest and king are distributed between the two sons at this blessing — the same division that governs all subsequent Yisra'ELi covenant politics.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 32 Yaakov at Bethel — The Tithe of Sons — The Angel of the Presence

1 And he dwelt that night at Bethel, and Levi dreamed that they had ordained and made him the priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever.

2 And he awoke from his sleep and blessed YHWH.

3 And Yaakov rose early in the morning, on the fourteenth of this month, and he gave a tithe of all that came with him, both of men and cattle, both of gold and every vessel and garment — yea, he gave tithes of all.

4 And in those days Rachel became sick, for she was with child, and Yaakov prayed for her.

5 And she gave birth to a son in the night, and called his name Ben-Oni, for she was in agony, but his father called his name Binyamin — the son of the right hand.

6 And Rachel died there, and she was buried in the land of Ephrath, that is Bethlehem.

7 And Yaakov built a pillar on the grave of Rachel, on the road above her grave.

8 And YHWH appeared to him in the night, and blessed him and said unto him: Your name shall not be called Yaakov, but Yisra'EL shall be your name.

9 And I am YHWH who created the heavens and the earth, I will increase you and multiply you exceedingly, and kings shall come forth from you, and they shall have dominion wherever the foot of the sons of men has trodden.

10 And I will give to your seed all the land under heaven, and they shall have dominion over all the nations as they desire, and afterwards they shall get possession of the whole earth and inherit it forever.

11 And he finished speaking with him, and he went up from him.

12 And Yaakov looked till he had gone up from him.

13 And he arose from that place happy and glad, and he set up a pillar of stone in that place — the pillar which he had set up — and he poured oil on it and sprinkled wine on it, and he called the name of that place Bethel.

14 And he consecrated it as an offering to YHWH.

15 And Yaakov again tithed all that he brought to Bethel of his servants, both cattle and sheep and goats, and he said to Levi: I have given you and your sons all the tithes.

16 And Levi ate there, he and his sons.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubf (4Q221) preserves portions of Chapter 32 in Hebrew. Levi's dream of the priestly ordination is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 32 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 32:1 — Levi dreamed that they had ordained and made him the priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever. The Levitical priesthood is established in Yovelim through a dream-revelation to Levi himself at Bethel. This parallels and precedes the formal Sinaitic ordination of Levi. The Aramaic Levi Document — found at Qumran (4Q213-214) — provides an extensive expansion of this Levi dream and the priestly instruction he receives, demonstrating that the Qumran community had an entire separate literary tradition around Levi as the prototype of the holy priesthood. The Levi tradition in Yovelim and the Aramaic Levi Document are interconnected texts.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 33 Reuven's Sin with Bilhah — The Law of Incest

1 And Yaakov went and dwelt to the south of Magdaladra'ef.

2 And he went to his father Yitzhak, he and Leah his wife, on the new moon of the tenth month.

3 And Reuven saw Bilhah, Rachel's maid, the concubine of his father, bathing in water in a secret place, and he loved her.

4 And he hid himself at night, and he entered the house of Bilhah at night, and he found her sleeping alone on a bed in her house.

5 And he lay with her, and she awoke and saw, and behold Reuven was lying with her in the bed, and she uncovered the border of his covering and seized him, and cried out, and discovered that it was Reuven.

6 And she was ashamed because of him, and released her hand from him, and he fled.

7 And she lamented greatly because of this thing, and did not tell it to any man.

8 And when Yaakov returned and sought her, she said unto him: I am not clean for you, for I have been defiled as regards you — for Reuven has defiled me, and has lain with me in the night, and I was asleep and did not discover it until he uncovered my skirt and slept with me.

9 And Yaakov was exceedingly wroth with Reuven because he had lain with Bilhah, because he had uncovered his father's skirt.

10 And Yaakov did not approach her again because Reuven had defiled her. And as for any man in Yisra'EL who uncovers his father's skirt — his deed is wicked exceedingly, for it is abominable before YHWH.

11 For this reason it is written and ordained on the heavenly tablets that a man should not lie with his father's wife, and should not uncover his father's skirt, for this is unclean — they shall surely die together, the man who lies with his father's wife and the woman also, for they have wrought uncleanness on the earth.

12 And there shall be nothing unclean before our Creator in the nation which he has chosen for himself as a possession.

13 And again, it is written a second time: Cursed be he who lies with the wife of his father, for he has uncovered the shame of his father — and all the holy ones of YHWH said: So be it, so be it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubf (4Q221) preserves portions of Chapter 33 in Hebrew.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 33 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Reuven-Bilhah narrative in Yovelim expands the brief notice of Bereshit 35:22 into a detailed account that establishes the incest law of Vayikra 18:8 as pre-Sinaitic covenant law written on the heavenly tablets. Yovelim's consistent method throughout is to find the historical event behind each covenant law — the law is not invented at Sinai but is the codification of what was already written on the heavenly tablets from the patriarchal period. Every law has a narrative occasion that preceded and prompted its inscription.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 34 The Sale of Yosef — Yaakov Mourns — Bilhah and Dinah Die

1 And in the first year of the fifth week of this jubilee his brothers seized Yosef and put him into a pit, and afterwards sold him to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver.

2 And they brought him into the land of Egypt, and sold him to Potifar, a eunuch of Par'oh, the chief of the cooks.

3 And the sons of Yaakov slaughtered a kid, and dipped the coat of Yosef in the blood, and sent it to Yaakov their father on the tenth of the seventh month.

4 And he mourned all that night, for they had brought it to him in the evening, and he became feverish with mourning for his death, and he said: An evil beast has devoured Yosef.

5 And all the members of his house sought to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted for his son.

6 And on that day Bilhah heard that Yosef had perished, and she died mourning him — and so she perished after Yosef.

7 And her sons Dan and Naftali mourned for their mother Bilhah, and afterwards for Yosef their brother.

10 And Yaakov remembered the day on which his father Yitzhak had made him fast when he had sold Esav's birthright for a mess of pottage, and from that day onwards he had kept this day each year, and this was the beginning of the Day of Atonement — for Yaakov had wept for Yosef on this very day.

History becomes liturgy through annual repetition on Yovelim's covenant calendar.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 36 The Death of Yitzhak — His Final Blessing and Commands

10 And if either of you devises evil against his brother, know that from henceforth everyone that devises evil against his brother shall fall into his hand, and shall be rooted out of the land of the living, and his seed shall be destroyed from under heaven.

11 But on the day of turbulence and execration and indignation and anger, with flaming devouring fire as he burnt Sodom, so likewise will he burn his land and his city and all that is his — and he shall be blotted out of the book of the discipline of the children of men, and not be recorded in the book of life, but in that which is appointed to destruction.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 36:11 — he shall be blotted out of the book of the discipline of the children of men, and not be recorded in the book of life, but in that which is appointed to destruction. The books of life and destruction appear in Yovelim as the definitive eschatological record — parallel to the heavenly tablets. Being written in the book of life is the covenant destiny of the righteous. Being blotted out of it and written in the book of destruction is the final verdict on those who devised evil against their brothers. Yitzhak invokes these books in his death-bed charge to Esav and Yaakov — the fraternal covenant between brothers is of cosmic and eternal consequence.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 44:4-7 — Yosef's declaration to his brothers: it was not you that sent me hither but YHWH. The covenant theology of Yovelim reaches its most precise statement in Yosef's revelation to his brothers. The brothers sold Yosef out of hatred and jealousy — their moral responsibility is real. 9 And these are the names of the sons of Yisra'EL who came into Egypt — Yaakov and his sons. Reuven the first-born of Yaakov.

10 And the sons of Reuven — Hanokh and Pallu and Hetzron and Karmi, four.

11 And the sons of Shim'on — Yemu'el and Yamin and Ohad and Yakhin and Tzohar and Sha'ul son of a Kena'anite woman, six.

12 And the sons of Levi — Gershon and Kehat and Merari, three.

13 And the sons of Yehudah — Er and Onan and Shelah and Perez and Zerah. But Er and Onan died in the land of Kena'an. And the sons of Perez were Hetzron and Hamul, five.

14 And the sons of Yissakhar — Tola and Puvah and Yov and Shimron, four.

15 And the sons of Zevulun — Sered and Elon and Yahleel, three.

16 These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore unto Yaakov in Paddan-Aram, with his daughter Dinah — all the souls of his sons and daughters were thirty-three.

17 And the sons of Gad — Tzifyon and Haggi, Shuni and Etzbon, Eri and Arodi and Areli, seven.

18 And the sons of Asher — Yimnah and Yishvah and Yishvi and Beriah and their sister Serah. And the sons of Beriah — Hever and Malkiel, two.

19 The daughters of Zilpah — these are the sons of Zilpah, whom Lavan gave to Leah his daughter, and these she bore unto Yaakov, sixteen souls.

20 The sons of Rachel the wife of Yaakov — Yosef and Binyamin.

21 And there were born to Yosef in the land of Egypt, before his father came into Egypt, those whom Asenath, the daughter of Potifar the priest of On, bore unto him, Menasheh and Efrayim, two souls.

22 And the sons of Binyamin — Bela and Bekher and Ashbel, Gera and Na'aman and Ehi and Rosh, Muppim and Huppim and Ard, ten.

23 All the souls of Rachel were fourteen souls.

24 And the sons of Bilhah, whom Lavan gave unto Rachel his daughter, and she bore to Yaakov — Dan and Naftali.

25 And the sons of Dan — Hushim, one soul.

26 And the sons of Naftali — Yahtze'el and Guni and Yetzer and Shillem, four souls.

27 All the souls of Bilhah were seven.

28 All the souls that came with Yaakov into Egypt were seventy souls. These are his children and his children's children, in all seventy, but five died in Egypt before Yosef — Er and Onan who died in the land of Kena'an, and Yosef's two sons, who were born in Egypt, make seventy.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserves portions of Chapter 45 in Hebrew.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 45 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The enumeration of all seventy souls who went down to Egypt with Yaakov is Yovelim's complete census of the covenant family at the moment of their descent. Seventy is the number of the nations listed in Bereshit 10 — the Table of Nations. The seventy souls of Yaakov's family entering Egypt mirrors the seventy nations of the world. Yovelim's numerology is deliberate: YHWH's covenant people enter Egypt as a complete and ordered family precisely numbered to correspond to the full scope of humanity. The covenant people are the counterweight to the nations — not a fragment but a complete covenantal unit.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 46 Yaakov Dies — Yosef Dies — The Bondage Begins

1 And Yisra'EL lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years, and all the days of the life of Yisra'EL were a hundred and forty-seven years, and Yisra'EL knew that he was going to die, and he called his son Yosef.

2 And he said to him: If I have found grace in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and swear to me by YHWH the Creator, that you will deal kindly and truly with me, and bury me not in Egypt — but carry me out of Egypt and bury me in the burial place of my fathers.

3 And Yosef said: I will do as you have said.

4 And Yisra'EL bowed himself upon the head of the bed.

5 And Yisra'EL called his sons before his death and said to them: I am going the way of my fathers — bury me in the double cave with Avraham and with Yitzhak.

6 And Yaakov died in the fortieth jubilee, in the second week of the sixth year thereof, and Yosef put his brothers and their father on the wagons of Par'oh to carry him to the land of Kena'an.

7 And Yosef buried his father in the double cave, in the mountain of Hebron.

8 And he returned to Egypt, and his brothers and all who had gone with him to bury their father.

9 And Yosef was at that time living and all the days of his life were a hundred and ten years. He died in the fifty-third week of years, in the six years thereof, in the second year of the forty-ninth jubilee.

10 And Yosef died after all his brothers, and he passed away in that year.

11 And the sons of Yisra'EL continued to multiply in the land of Egypt and became a great nation.

12 And there arose a new king in Egypt who did not know Yosef, and he ruled over all Egypt and was very wicked towards the children of Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserves portions of Chapter 46 in Hebrew.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 46 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 46:12 — there arose a new king who did not know Yosef, and he was very wicked towards the children of Yisra'EL. Yovelim's closing of the Yosef narrative with the beginning of the Egyptian bondage connects the end of the patriarchal period to the opening of the Shemot narrative. The book of Yovelim moves from creation through the full patriarchal genealogy and arrives at the threshold of the slavery that will produce the Exodus — the defining covenant event of Yisra'ELi history. The covenant family has descended to Egypt; now the covenant rescue will rise from Egypt.

MOSHE AND THE EXODUS — CHAPTERS 47 THROUGH 50

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 47 The Birth of Moshe — Miriam's Song — Moshe in Par'oh's House

1 And in the seventh week, in the seventh year, in the forty-seventh jubilee, your father went forth from the land of Kena'an, and you were born in the fourth week, in the sixth year thereof, in the forty-eighth jubilee — this was a time of affliction for the children of Yisra'EL.

2 And Par'oh, king of Egypt, issued a command regarding them that they should cast all their male children which were born into the river.

3 And they cast them in for seven months until the day that you were born.

4 And your mother hid you for three months, and they told regarding her.

5 And she made an ark for you, and covered it with pitch and asphalt, and placed it in the flags by the bank of the river, and she placed you in it seven days, and your mother came by night and suckled you.

6 And by day Miriam your sister guarded you from the birds.

7 In those days Tayis, the daughter of Par'oh, came to bathe in the river, and she heard your voice crying.

8 And she told her maidens to bring you forth, and they brought you to her.

9 And she took you out of the ark, and she had compassion on you.

10 And your sister said to her: Shall I go and call to you one of the Hebrew women to nurse the child for you?

11 And she said: Go.

12 And she went and called your mother Yokheved, and she gave her wages, and she nursed you.

13 And afterwards, when you had grown up, they brought you to the daughter of Par'oh, and you became her son, and Amram your father taught you writing.

14 And after you had completed three weeks of years, Moshe went and killed the Egyptian who had struck and killed an Israelite, and hid him in the sand.

15 And he fled from Egypt in the first week of the forty-ninth jubilee, and he dwelt in the land of Midian five weeks and one year, and he returned to Egypt in the second week, in the second year, in the fiftieth jubilee.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QJub (1Q17-18) and 4QJubh (4Q223-224) preserve portions of Chapter 47 in Hebrew. The detailed account of Moshe's infancy and the naming of Miriam as his guardian at the river are attested in the Cave manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 47 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 47:13 — Amram your father taught you writing. Yovelim explicitly attributes Moshe's literacy to his father Amram's teaching — not to his Egyptian education. The covenant scribal tradition runs: Chanokh the first scribe, Noakh who wrote the healing book, Avraham who sent written letters, Yaakov who learned to write while Esav did not, and now Amram teaching his son Moshe. The man who will write the Torah down on tablets is trained in writing by his covenant father within the enslaved community in Egypt. The power of the written covenant word begins not in Par'oh's palace but in the house of Amram the Levite.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 48 The Return to Egypt — Mastema and the Plagues — The Passover Night

1 And in the second week of the second year, in the fiftieth jubilee, you came and stood before Par'oh.

2 And YHWH showed his signs before him and was not ashamed, and before his servants, and before all the people of Egypt.

3 And YHWH worked his signs in the land of Egypt, and YHWH smote the Egyptians and their streams.

4 And the blood and the frogs and the gnats and the flies and the murrain of the cattle and the hail and the locusts and the darkness and the death of the firstborn — these are the signs and wonders which YHWH did before Par'oh and before all the Egyptians.

5 And YHWH did everything for the sake of Yisra'EL, and according to his covenant which he had ordained with Avraham that he would take them and bring them out, and bring them into the land which he had promised to Avraham that he would give it to him and to his seed after him.

6 And on the fourteenth day of the first month YHWH smote all the firstborn of Egypt.

7 And Mastema led on the Egyptians and hardened their hearts, and made them strong, that they should hold fast to the pursuit of the children of Yisra'EL.

8 For Mastema desired that all the children of Yisra'EL should be destroyed, and that Egypt should be established, and that the pursuit after them should be continued until they should all be drowned in the sea.

9 But on that night we guarded the children of Yisra'EL, that the angel of the presence should not smite any of them — and YHWH made the face of the Egyptians strong and hardened their hearts.

10 And YHWH executed his judgments on all the gods of Egypt, and burnt them with fire.

11 And everything was sent through his hands — all the plagues we brought against Par'oh and against all Egypt.

12 And YHWH our Creator revenged himself on them for all the evil which they wrought against Yisra'EL.

13 And for all the signs and wonders which he performed in Egypt and in the Red Sea, YHWH saved Yisra'EL from the hand of Egypt and from the hand of all peoples.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QJub (1Q17-18) preserves portions of Chapter 48 in Hebrew. Mastema's role in hardening Par'oh's heart and leading the Egyptian pursuit is attested in the Cave 1 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 48 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 48:7-8 — Mastema led on the Egyptians and hardened their hearts, for Mastema desired that all the children of Yisra'EL should be destroyed. Yovelim assigns the hardening of Par'oh's heart to Mastema — the chief of the evil spirits from Chapter 10. YHWH uses Mastema's own hostility against the covenant people as an instrument of the final judgment on Egypt. Mastema wants Yisra'EL destroyed — YHWH uses that desire to drive Egypt into the sea. The adversarial force that seeks the covenant people's destruction becomes the mechanism of YHWH's deliverance. This is the consistent pattern of Yovelim's providential theology: YHWH turns the enemy's own plans into the instrument of their defeat.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 49 The Passover — Its Laws and Its Memorial Forever

1 Remember the commandment which YHWH commanded you concerning the Passover, that you should celebrate it in its season on the fourteenth of the first month — that you should kill it before it is evening, and that they should eat it by night on the evening of the fifteenth from the time of the setting of the sun.

2 For on this night — the beginning of the festival and the beginning of the joy — you were eating the Passover in Egypt, when all the powers of Mastema had been let loose to slay all the first-born in the land of Egypt.

3 And YHWH our Creator slew them all — the great and small, the cattle and the men and the dogs, and all the first-born of Egypt, and Par'oh on his throne.

4 And all the Egyptians arose and cried with a loud voice, for all the first-born of Egypt were slain, and there was not a house in Egypt wherein there was not one dead.

5 And all the children of Yisra'EL were safe, and there was not a single dog that moved its tongue against any child of Yisra'EL.

6 And there was a great night of darkness, and a great night of blessing — two days and two nights.

7 And all the children of Yisra'EL were eating the flesh of the paschal lamb, and drinking the wine, and were lauding and blessing and giving thanks to YHWH the Creator of their fathers.

8 And they were ready to go forth from under the yoke of Egypt, and from the evil bondage.

9 And remember this day all the days of your life, and observe it from year to year all the days of your life, once a year, on its day, according to all the law thereof, and do not adjourn it from day to day nor from month to month.

10 For it is an eternal ordinance, and engraved on the heavenly tablets regarding all the children of Yisra'EL that they should observe it every year on its day once a year, throughout all their generations — there is no limit of days, for this is ordained forever.

11 And the man who is free from uncleanness, and does not come to observe it on occasion of its day, so as to bring an acceptable offering before YHWH, and to eat and to drink before YHWH on the day of its festival, that man who is clean and close at hand — if he fails to observe it, he shall be cut off.

12 And if any man who is unclean or on a far journey, if he observes the Passover in the second month — they shall eat it at sunset, on the fifteenth day of that month, with unleavened bread and bitter herbs, and shall drink wine.

13 And they shall not leave any of it until the morning, and none of it shall be broken — according to all the law of the first Passover they shall observe it.

14 And all who are assembled and who live near the sanctuary of YHWH — let there be no Passover observed at a distance from his sanctuary, but let them all come before YHWH and observe it in its season, and everything that is outside of the law is unclean before YHWH.

15 And you, Moshe, command the children of Yisra'EL to observe the ordinances of the Passover, as it was commanded to you — declare you unto them every year and the day of its days, and the festival of unleavened bread, that they should eat unleavened bread seven days.

16 And that they should observe its festival, and that they should bring an oblation every day during those seven days of joy before YHWH on the altar of your Creator.

17 For you celebrated this feast with haste when you went forth from Egypt, till the day when you passed over the sea, and into the wilderness of Shur — for on the shore of the sea you completed it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QJub (1Q17-18) and 2QJub (2Q19-20) preserve portions of Chapter 49 in Hebrew. The Passover law chapter is one of the most extensively attested passages of Yovelim in the Cave manuscripts. The Qumran community's strict Passover observance — including the date, the sanctuary requirement, and the prohibition on observing it at a distance — reflects direct dependence on Yovelim's legislation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 49 in full.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 49:10 — the Passover is an eternal ordinance engraved on the heavenly tablets regarding all the children of Yisra'EL — there is no limit of days. Yovelim's Passover legislation, like all its covenant laws, grounds the commandment in the heavenly tablets. The Passover is not a humanly instituted memorial — it is a divinely prescribed eternal covenant observance whose authority transcends the Sinaitic legislation. It was being observed before Sinai by the act of eating the lamb in Egypt, and it will be observed forever after because it is engraved in the heavenly tablets without a time limit.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 49:14 — let there be no Passover observed at a distance from the sanctuary of YHWH — all must come before YHWH and observe it in its season. The centralization requirement for Passover — observe it only at the sanctuary, not in individual households — is one of the most distinctive halakhic positions of Yovelim. This aligns with Devarim 16:2-6's requirement that Passover be observed at the place YHWH chooses. The Qumran community's interpretation of this law shaped their understanding of when and how Passover could be legitimately observed in the absence of the Temple.

YOVELIM — CHAPTER 50 The Sabbath Laws — The Jubilee Structure — The Final Command

1 And after this law I gave you the command of the Sabbaths of the land, and the jubilees in the years of weeks and the jubilees I shall declare unto you till the land shall rest — for it has rested from you for ten weeks of years.

2 And this entire history I have written for you and the Sabbaths of the years I have reported them all — and the years of the jubilees from the day of creation till the day when the heaven and the earth shall be renewed, and all their creation, according to YHWH's commandment, shall be renewed.

3 In those days we will make the sanctuary of YHWH, and will come to Zion with joy — and all the beloved ones will rejoice, and there will be great joy, and the holy ones and the sons of YHWH will be honored.

4 And now, I have written down this law for you and ordained it concerning the Sabbath days throughout the jubilees.

5 And all the Sabbaths I have written for you — six Sabbaths shall you work, but on the seventh Sabbath is the day of the holy Shabbat of YHWH — do no work thereon.

6 Whoever does any work on this day, whoever kindles a fire, whoever rides on any beast, whoever travels by ship on the sea, whoever strikes or kills anything, whoever draws water, whoever carries any burden, whoever goes to war on this day — that man shall die.

7 So that the children of Yisra'EL shall observe this day throughout their generations, and not be rooted out of the land — for it is a holy day and a blessed day.

8 And everyone who observes it and rests thereon from all his work, will be holy and blessed throughout all days like unto us.

9 Declare and say to the children of Yisra'EL the law of this day both that they should rest on it, and that they should not forsake it in the error of their hearts.

10 And that it is not lawful to do any work thereon which is unseemly — to do thereon their own pleasure, and not to prepare thereon anything to be eaten or drunk, and not to draw water, and not to bring in or take out thereon through their gates any burden — which had not been prepared or made ready on the sixth day.

11 And they shall not bring in nor take out from house to house on that day — for that day is more holy and blessed than any jubilee day of the jubilees — on this we kept Shabbat in the heavens before it was made known to any flesh to keep Shabbat thereon on the earth.

12 And the Creator of all things blessed this day which he had created for blessing and holiness and glory above all days.

13 This law and testimony was given to the children of Yisra'EL as a law forever unto their generations.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 2QJub (2Q19-20) and 11QJub (11Q12) preserve portions of Chapter 50 in Hebrew. The final Shabbat law chapter — the closing chapter of Yovelim — is attested in the Cave 2 and Cave 11 manuscripts. The book that opened with the Sinai covenant closes with the Shabbat as the eternal sign that encompasses and seals all other covenant laws.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 50 in full, completing all 50 chapters of the Book of Yovelim.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 50:2 — this entire history I have written for you, and the Sabbaths of the years I have reported them all — and the years of the jubilees from the day of creation till the day when the heaven and the earth shall be renewed. The final chapter of Yovelim places the entire book within the cosmic time frame that it has constructed throughout: from the first day of creation to the renewal of heaven and earth, all of time is structured in jubilees and Sabbath years, and all of it has been written. The angel of the presence has dictated the complete record to Moshe, from Adam to the threshold of the Promised Land.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 50:11 — this day is more holy and blessed than any jubilee day of the jubilees — on this we kept Shabbat in the heavens before it was made known to any flesh to keep Shabbat thereon on the earth. The final theological statement of Yovelim places the Shabbat above even the jubilee year — the governing structure of the entire book. The Shabbat is the most holy day in all of sacred time. It was kept in heaven by the angels before it was given to humanity. When Yisra'EL keeps Shabbat on earth, they participate in the oldest and most fundamental covenant observance in existence — one that predates human history and will endure past the renewal of creation. The Book of Yovelim ends where it began: with the Shabbat as the sign of the covenant between YHWH and all creation.
